claim: Vitamin D deficiency is unrelated to birth weight.

result.claim: Vitamin D deficiency is unrelated to birth weight.
result.reasoning: The claim that "Vitamin D deficiency is unrelated to birth weight" is strongly refuted by a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies (Paper ID: 2425364). This paper, titled "Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes," explicitly states that "Insufficient serum levels of 25-OHD were associated with small for gestational age infants (pooled odds ratio 1.85, 95% confidence interval 1.52 to 2.26)" (Proposition ID: prop_1513). Furthermore, the abstract concludes that "Pregnant women with low 25-OHD levels had an increased risk of bacterial vaginosis and lower birth weight infants, but not delivery by caesarean section" (Chunk ID: chunk_45). This evidence directly demonstrates a relationship between maternal vitamin D deficiency and lower birth weight, contradicting the claim.

evidence.text: Studies involving five categories of birth weight identified odds ratios (ORs) of 1.15 (95% CI 1.04 to 1.26) for 3,500 g to 3,999 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: The studies used a birth weight of 2,500 to 2,599 g as a reference.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: Birth size might be a correlate of subsequent breast cancer risk.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Birth weight was positively associated with breast cancer risk in studies based on birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: In birth record data, p for linear trend = 0.001 in those who weighed >= 4.000 kg.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Participants were divided into groups according to serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of <10 ng/mL, 10-18 ng/mL, and >18 ng/mL.
evidence.source: Relationship between serum parathyroid hormone levels, vitamin D sufficiency, and calcium intake.

evidence.text: Insufficient serum levels of 25-OHD were associated with small for gestational age infants (pooled odds ratio 1.85, 95% confidence interval 1.52 to 2.26).
evidence.source: Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.

evidence.text: Studies involving five categories of birth weight identified odds ratios (ORs) of 1.24 (95% confidence interval [CI] 1.04 to 1.48) for 4,000 g or more.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: Insufficient serum levels of 25-OHD were associated with gestational diabetes (pooled odds ratio 1.49, 95% confidence interval 1.18 to 1.89).
evidence.source: Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.

evidence.text: Genetic influences on body-mass index contribute to the similarity of the body-mass index of twins later in life.
evidence.source: The body-mass index of twins who have been reared apart.

evidence.text: Studies based on two birth weight categories showed an odds ratio (OR) of 1.09 (95% CI 1.02 to 1.18) for > or =3,000 g relative to <3,000 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: Serum PTH was lowest in the group with a serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of more than 18 ng/mL.
evidence.source: Relationship between serum parathyroid hormone levels, vitamin D sufficiency, and calcium intake.

evidence.text: At a calcium intake of more than 1200 mg/d, there was a significant difference between the lowest and highest vitamin D groups.
evidence.source: Relationship between serum parathyroid hormone levels, vitamin D sufficiency, and calcium intake.

evidence.text: A high birth weight (> or =4000 g) in 2 successive births was associated with an increased risk of breast cancer before adjusting for placental weight and other covariates.
evidence.source: Pregnancy characteristics and maternal risk of breast cancer.

evidence.text: There was a strong relation between the weight class of the adoptees and the body-mass index of their biologic mothers, P less than 0.0001.
evidence.source: An adoption study of human obesity.

evidence.text: Increased birthweight was associated with a relative risk of 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21) for breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: The childhood environment has little or no influence on the body-mass index of twins later in life.
evidence.source: The body-mass index of twins who have been reared apart.

evidence.text: Pooled relative risk per one standard deviation increment in birth weight was 1.06 in studies based on birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The study aimed to assess the effect of 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25-OHD) levels on pregnancy outcomes and birth variables.
evidence.source: Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.

evidence.text: Studies based on three birth weight categories showed an odds ratio (OR) of 1.15 (95% CI 1.01 to 1.31) for > or =4,000 g relative to <3,000 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review
